name: Leonese

ancestors.notes: Common Asturleonese trunk. Leonese diverged southward during the Reconquista, developing its own written tradition in the Kingdom of León.
Celtic substrate of the Asturian-Leonese highlands, shared with central Asturian.
Suebi superstrate, somewhat lighter in León than in the Asturian coast.
Visigothic superstrate: the Leonese meseta was more thoroughly Visigothic than coastal Asturias.
Andalusi Arabic: more intense in León than in Asturias, given the longer Moorish occupation of the meseta and the Reconquista repopulation from south.
Mozarabic adstrate: significant in the repopulation of the Leonese meseta, as Mozarabic-speaking Christians from al-Andalus joined the Reconquista settlements.

notes: Medieval Leonese (llingua llionesa / leonés), the Asturleonese variety of the Kingdom of León (~10th–15th c. CE). Attested: Fuero de Avilés (1085), Fuero Juzgo leonés, Fuero de Zamora, and extensive notarial documents from León, Zamora, Salamanca, and the Trás-os-Montes border region. KEY FEATURES distinguishing Leonese from modern central Asturian: (1) 4-WAY SIBILANT CONTRAST preserved: voiceless apical /s̺/ ⟨-ss-/s-⟩ vs. voiced apical /z̺/ ⟨-s-⟩ vs. voiceless predorsal /t͡s/ ⟨ç,c-⟩ vs. voiced predorsal /d͡z/ ⟨z⟩ — identical to contemporary Galaico-Portuguese. (2) ⟨lh⟩ = /ʎ/ digraph (alongside ⟨ll⟩) in southern Leonese and border documents under Portuguese influence. (3) ⟨nh⟩ = /ɲ/ similarly. (4) Intervocalic -l- and -n- PRESERVED (unlike Portuguese: 'salir' not *'sair', 'lhana' not *'lã'). (5) F- initial preserved throughout. (6) No vowel reduction. (7) Leonese diphthongs: ie, ue inherited from ast. This variety is the immediate ancestor of ast-PT-x-medieval and the modern Mirandese-group languages. Sources: Menéndez Pidal (1906), Staaff (1907), Williams (1962), Martínez Álvarez (1967).

sources.author: García Arias, X.Ll.
sources.title: Gramática histórica de la lengua asturiana

sources.author: Penny, R.
sources.title: A History of the Spanish Language
